For a complete contribution, the authors should include a counterexample demonstrating a task or data distribution where Flamingo's few-shot adaptation breaks down. For instance, they could construct a synthetic VQA dataset requiring precise counting of objects beyond 5, showing performance collapse as the number of objects exceeds the training context length. This would demonstrate the non-vacuous nature of their claims and help readers understand the limitations of the approach.

- Unverified recovery of baseline models: The paper claims to generalize existing approaches but never verifies that baseline models are recovered as special cases. For example, when the cross-attention layers are removed and only text inputs are used, Flamingo should reduce to the original Chinchilla language model. The authors should include an experiment showing identical outputs on text-only tasks between Flamingo (with visual components disabled) and the frozen Chinchilla model. Similarly, they should demonstrate that with single-image input and appropriate masking, the model recovers standard image-text retrieval behavior comparable to CLIP.

- Missing inference implementation guidance: For practical adoption, the paper needs concrete implementation details for few-shot inference. The authors should provide pseudocode showing how to construct prompts for different tasks, handle variable-length visual sequences, and implement the per-image attention masking during decoding. Specifically, they should include code examples for: (a) building a 32-shot prompt for VQA, (b) computing attention masks for interleaved sequences, and (c) beam search with visual conditioning. Without this, researchers cannot reproduce the few-shot results despite having the model architecture.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-evaluate results separating potentially contaminated development benchmarks from truly held-out benchmarks, with clear labeling of each category

- Add experiments validating the claim about handling arbitrarily interleaved sequences, particularly performance with increasing numbers of images beyond the 5-image training limit

- Provide comprehensive computational analysis of few-shot inference costs compared to fine-tuning, including latency and memory requirements

- Conduct systematic analysis of failure modes including hallucination rates, object binding issues, and negation handling with concrete examples

- Document data quality assessment procedures for web-scraped datasets and analyze their impact on few-shot learning performance

comments.title: Contradiction about frozen vision backbone
comments.quote: While not the main focus of our work, we verify that when given more data, Flamingo models can be adapted to a task by fine-tuning their weights. In Table 2, we explore fine-tuning our largest model, Flamingo, for a given task with no limit on the annotation budget. In short, we do so by fine-tuning the model on a short schedule with a small learning rate by additionally unfreezing the vision backbone to accommodate a higher input resolution (details in Appendix B.2.2).
comments.explanation: This contradicts the core architectural claim from Section 2 that Flamingo keeps pretrained models frozen. The approach section states the vision encoder remains frozen, yet this fine-tuning section explicitly mentions 'unfreezing the vision backbone.' This creates an internal inconsistency about whether the vision components truly remain frozen during adaptation. The authors should clarify that unfreezing only occurs during fine-tuning (not few-shot adaptation) and emphasize that the architectural contribution assumes frozen components for few-shot learning.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated generalization claim
comments.quote: This single-image cross-attention scheme importantly allows the model to seamlessly generalise to any number of visual inputs, regardless of how many are used during training. In particular, we use only up to 5 images per sequence when training on our interleaved datasets, yet our model is able to benefit from sequences of up to 32 pairs (or “shots”) of images/videos and corresponding texts during evaluation.
comments.explanation: The claim that the model 'seamlessly generalises to any number of visual inputs' is presented as an architectural property but lacks formal justification. The text states training uses up to 5 images while evaluation uses up to 32, but provides no mathematical argument showing why the per-image masking scheme enables this extrapolation. The dependency on previous images via LM self-attention is mentioned, but no analysis shows this preserves performance when scaling beyond the training distribution. Add empirical validation showing performance as a function of number of images, particularly beyond the 5-image training limit.

comments.title: Inconsistent benchmark counting
comments.quote: We notably reserve a large set of held-out benchmarks which have not been used for validation of any design decisions or hyperparameters of the approach. We use these to estimate unbiased few-shot performance. (iii) Flamingo sets a new state of the art in few-shot learning on a wide array of 16 multimodal language and image/video understanding tasks.
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency between claiming to reserve 'a large set of held-out benchmarks' for unbiased evaluation and then reporting SOTA on '16 multimodal' tasks. The paper indicates 5 benchmarks (COCO, OKVQA, VQAv2, MSVDQA, VATEX) were used for validation during development. If the 16-task total includes these 5 potentially contaminated benchmarks, then the claim about using held-out benchmarks for unbiased estimation is misleading. The authors should clarify how many of the 16 tasks are truly held-out versus those used during validation.

comments.title: Mathematical notation inconsistency in likelihood equation
comments.quote:
$p(y|x)=\prod_{\ell=1}^{L}p(y_{\ell}|y_{<\ell},x_{\leq\ell}),$ (1)

where $y_{\ell}$ is the $\ell$-th language token of the input text, $y_{<\ell}$ is the set of preceding tokens, $x_{\leq\ell}$ is the set of images/videos preceding token $y_{\ell}$ in the interleaved sequence and $p$ is parametrized by a Flamingo model.
comments.explanation: The notation in Equation (1) is inconsistent with standard autoregressive formulation. The left side uses $p(y|x)$ suggesting $y$ is the entire text sequence, but the right side product goes from $\ell=1$ to $L$ where $L$ is undefined. If $L$ represents the length of text $y$, then the left side should be $p(y_1, \ldots, y_L|x)$ to be mathematically precise. Additionally, the conditioning on $x_{\leq\ell}$ creates a non-standard autoregressive dependency where each token depends on different subsets of visual inputs, which needs clarification about how this differs from standard sequence modeling.

comments.title: COCO captioning scores exceed maximum possible
comments.explanation: The COCO captioning score of 106.3 for Flamingo-9B with 32 shots appears problematic. While CIDEr scores can exceed 100, the dramatic jump from 99.0 (Flamingo-3B) to 106.3, combined with the fine-tuned SOTA at 143.3, raises questions about metric consistency. The authors should clarify whether all COCO scores use the same metric and verify these calculations, as such large improvements between model sizes are unusual for captioning tasks.

comments.title: Inconsistent vision encoder description
comments.quote: Our vision encoder is a pretrained and frozen Normalizer-Free ResNet (NFNet) [10] – we use the F6 model. We pretrain the vision encoder using a contrastive objective on our datasets of image and text pairs, using the two-term contrastive loss from Radford et al. [85].
comments.explanation: The text states the vision encoder is 'pretrained and frozen' but then immediately says 'We pretrain the vision encoder using a contrastive objective.' This is contradictory - if the encoder is pretrained, it should already have been trained before use in Flamingo. The authors likely mean they use an NFNet-F6 that was pretrained on ImageNet, then they further pretrained it on their multimodal data using contrastive learning before freezing it for the main Flamingo training. This distinction matters for understanding the training pipeline.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about arbitrarily interleaved data
comments.quote: We propose key architectural innovations to: (i) bridge powerful pretrained vision-only and language-only models, (ii) handle sequences of arbitrarily interleaved visual and textual data, and (iii) seamlessly ingest images or videos as inputs. Thanks to their flexibility, Flamingo models can be trained on large-scale multimodal web corpora containing arbitrarily interleaved text and images, which is key to endow them with in-context few-shot learning capabilities.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the model can handle 'arbitrarily interleaved visual and textual data' and is trained on 'arbitrarily interleaved text and images.' However, the models were only trained on sequences with up to 5 images. The term 'arbitrarily' is misleading since there's a concrete limit during training. The claim should be qualified to reflect this limitation, such as 'sequences of interleaved visual and textual data with up to 5 images during training.'

comments.title: Contradictory fine-tuning performance claim
comments.quote: Interestingly, fine-tuning our pretrained LM also leads to a drop in performance of $-8.0\%$. This indicates an instance of “catastrophic forgetting” *[71]*, in which the model progressively forgets its pretraining while training on a new objective.
comments.explanation: The claim that fine-tuning causes 'catastrophic forgetting' appears contradictory to the experimental setup. If the model is fine-tuned on multimodal data while maintaining access to the original pretraining data (as suggested by mentioning 'training with the pre-training dataset (MassiveText) in the mixture'), then this is not classic catastrophic forgetting. Classic catastrophic forgetting occurs when a model learns new tasks without access to original training data. The authors should clarify whether fine-tuning was done WITHOUT access to MassiveText, or reframe this as 'interference' between objectives.

comments.title: Inconsistent model size naming
comments.quote:
We perform experiments across three models sizes, building on the 1.4B, 7B, and 70B parameter Chinchilla models [42]; calling them respectively Flamingo-3B, Flamingo-9B and

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Flamingo-80B. For brevity, we refer to the last as Flamingo throughout the paper.
comments.explanation: There is an inconsistency in the model naming convention. The text states the models are built on 1.4B, 7B, and 70B parameter Chinchilla models, but then calls them Flamingo-3B, Flamingo-9B, and Flamingo-80B respectively. The parameter counts don't match (1.4B → 3B, 7B → 9B, 70B → 80B). This discrepancy needs clarification - either the base Chinchilla sizes are incorrect, or the Flamingo naming is wrong, or there's an explanation missing about why the parameter counts differ.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated optimization strategy claim
comments.quote: We accumulate gradients over all datasets, which we found outperforms a “round-robin” approach *[17]*.
comments.explanation: The authors claim gradient accumulation outperforms round-robin training but provide no evidence. The citation [17] may not directly support this specific comparison for their multimodal setup. This claim should either be removed or supported with an ablation study showing the performance difference between the two approaches on their specific datasets and model architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguous temporal embedding application
comments.quote: For video inputs, frames are sampled at 1 FPS and encoded independently to obtain a 3D spatio-temporal grid of features to which learned temporal embeddings are added.
comments.explanation: The description of temporal embeddings is ambiguous. It's unclear whether these embeddings are added before or after flattening the 3D grid to 1D, and whether they are added to each frame's features or across the temporal dimension. This affects how the Perceiver Resampler processes video features. The authors should specify: 'learned temporal embeddings are added to each frame's feature map before flattening' or 'temporal embeddings are added along the time dimension after flattening'.

comments.title: Missing architectural detail for latent queries
comments.quote: Similar to Perceiver [48] and DETR [13], we learn a predefined number of latent input queries which are fed to a Transformer and cross-attend to the visual features.
comments.explanation: The description omits a key architectural detail: the dimensionality of the latent queries and how they are initialized. Since these queries produce the final 64 visual tokens, their dimension determines the cross-attention key/value dimensions in the language model. The authors should state: 'we learn 64 latent queries of dimension D (where D matches the language model's hidden size)' or specify the actual dimension used.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Flamingo, a family of visual language models that achieve strong few-shot learning performance across a wide range of image and video tasks. The key architectural innovations—using frozen pretrained vision and language models bridged by a Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention layers—are clearly motivated and ablated. The empirical results are impressive, with Flamingo often matching or exceeding fine-tuned state-of-the-art models using only 32 examples. The paper is well-written and the experiments are thorough, making a significant contribution to multimodal few-shot learning.

Overview issues:

- Limited analysis of failure cases and task-specific weaknesses: The paper reports strong average performance but provides almost no analysis of where or why Flamingo fails. For example, the ablation study (Table 3) shows that removing the interleaved M3W dataset causes a 17% drop in overall score, but there is no discussion of which specific tasks or types of visual reasoning suffer most. Similarly, the paper notes that Flamingo lags behind contrastive models on classification (Section 5) but does not analyze whether this is due to the generative objective, the frozen vision encoder, or the Perceiver bottleneck. Without such analysis, it is difficult to assess the model's true generality and to identify directions for improvement. The authors should add a systematic analysis of failure cases, perhaps by breaking down performance by task category (e.g., counting, spatial reasoning, attribute recognition) and by comparing to a fine-tuned baseline on those categories.

- Sensitivity to prompt design and example ordering is not addressed: The paper acknowledges that in-context learning is sensitive to the choice and ordering of examples (Section 5), but it does not provide any empirical investigation of this sensitivity for Flamingo. The few-shot results in Table 1 are reported for a single prompt template and example set, so it is unclear how robust the reported numbers are. Given that the model is intended for practical use by non-experts, understanding this sensitivity is critical. The authors should include experiments that vary the prompt template, the number and ordering of shots, and the selection of support examples (e.g., random vs. stratified), reporting variance across multiple runs. This would strengthen the claim that Flamingo provides reliable few-shot adaptation.

- Potential information bottleneck in the Perceiver Resampler is not explored: The Perceiver Resampler compresses a variable number of visual features into a fixed set of 64 tokens. While this is motivated by computational efficiency, the paper does not investigate whether this bottleneck loses information that is important for fine-grained tasks like TextVQA or HatefulMemes. The ablation in Table 3 (row vi) compares the Perceiver to an MLP and a Transformer but does not vary the number of latent queries. If the model's performance on certain tasks is limited by the number of visual tokens, this would be an important architectural limitation. The authors should include an ablation that varies the number of latent queries (e.g., 32, 64, 128) and reports performance on tasks that require fine-grained visual detail, such as TextVQA and HatefulMemes.

- Unclear generalization to distribution shift and out-of-distribution data: The paper trains on web data and evaluates on standard benchmarks, but it does not test how Flamingo handles distribution shift—for example, images from a different domain (e.g., medical, satellite) or tasks that require specialized knowledge. The few-shot learning claim implies that the model can adapt to novel tasks, but all evaluated tasks are standard vision-language benchmarks that are likely well-represented in the training data. Without experiments on out-of-distribution tasks or datasets, it is hard to know whether Flamingo's few-shot ability is genuine adaptation or simply retrieval of memorized patterns. The authors should include at least one experiment on a held-out task or domain that is not well-covered in the training data, such as a specialized VQA dataset (e.g., VizWiz already tests a different domain, but more could be done) or a task with novel visual concepts.

- Lack of comparison to parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods: The paper compares Flamingo's few-shot performance to models that are fully fine-tuned on thousands of examples, but it does not compare to parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (e.g., adapter modules, LoRA, prefix tuning) that also aim to adapt large pretrained models with few examples. Such methods are relevant because they offer an alternative to in-context learning and may achieve better performance in low-data regimes. Including comparisons to these methods would better contextualize Flamingo's contributions and help practitioners choose between approaches. The authors should add a comparison to at least one parameter-efficient fine-tuning method (e.g., LoRA applied to a vision-language model) on a subset of the benchmarks.

- No analysis of Perceiver Resampler information bottleneck: The Perceiver Resampler compresses visual features into 64 tokens, but the paper never investigates whether this fixed-size bottleneck loses information critical for fine-grained tasks like TextVQA or HatefulMemes. The ablation in Table 3 (row vi) compares the Perceiver to an MLP and a Transformer but does not vary the number of latent queries. If performance on certain tasks is limited by the number of visual tokens, this is an important architectural limitation. The authors should include an ablation that varies the number of latent queries (e.g., 32, 64, 128) and reports performance on tasks requiring fine-grained visual detail, such as TextVQA and HatefulMemes.

- No evaluation on out-of-distribution tasks or domains: The paper trains on web data and evaluates on standard benchmarks, but it does not test how Flamingo handles distribution shift—for example, images from a different domain (e.g., medical, satellite) or tasks requiring specialized knowledge. The few-shot learning claim implies the model can adapt to novel tasks, but all evaluated tasks are standard vision-language benchmarks likely well-represented in the training data. Without experiments on out-of-distribution tasks or datasets, it is hard to know whether Flamingo's few-shot ability is genuine adaptation or retrieval of memorized patterns. The authors should include at least one experiment on a held-out task or domain not well-covered in the training data, such as a specialized VQA dataset (e.g., medical VQA) or a task with novel visual concepts.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a systematic analysis of failure cases, breaking down performance by task category (e.g., counting, spatial reasoning, attribute recognition) and comparing to a fine-tuned baseline.

- Include experiments on prompt sensitivity, varying the prompt template, number/ordering of shots, and selection of support examples, reporting variance across multiple runs.

- Ablate the number of latent queries in the Perceiver Resampler (e.g., 32, 64, 128) and report performance on fine-grained tasks like TextVQA and HatefulMemes.

- Add at least one experiment on an out-of-distribution task or domain to test genuine few-shot generalization.

- Compare Flamingo to parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (e.g., LoRA) on a subset of benchmarks.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about surpassing fine-tuned SOTA
comments.quote: Of the 16 tasks we consider, Flamingo also surpasses the fine-tuned state of the art on 6 tasks, despite using orders of magnitude less task-specific training data (see Figure 2).
comments.explanation: This claim is ambiguous. It could mean Flamingo's few-shot performance beats fine-tuned models on 6 tasks, or that Flamingo (with or without fine-tuning) surpasses fine-tuned SOTA on 6 tasks. The later paragraph says fine-tuning Flamingo sets new SOTA on 5 additional benchmarks, which muddles the picture. The authors should rephrase to explicitly state: 'Flamingo, using only few-shot prompting, surpasses the fine-tuned state of the art on 6 of 16 tasks.'

comments.title: Inconsistent count of SOTA achievements
comments.quote: ) Flamingo sets a new state of the art in few-shot learning on a wide array of 16 multimodal language and image/video understanding tasks. On 6 of these 16 tasks, Flamingo also outperforms the fine-tuned state of the art despite using only 32 task-specific examples, around 1000 times less task-specific training data than the current state of the art. With a larger annotation budget, Flamingo can also be effectively fine-tuned to set a new state of the art on five additional challenging benchmarks: VQAv2, VATEX, V
comments.explanation: The paragraph first says Flamingo sets a new few-shot SOTA on 16 tasks. Then it says on 6 of those 16, Flamingo outperforms the fine-tuned SOTA. Then it says fine-tuning sets new SOTA on 5 additional benchmarks. The total tasks where Flamingo achieves some form of SOTA appears to be 6 + 5 = 11, not 16. The paper needs to reconcile these numbers by explicitly distinguishing between 'few-shot SOTA' and 'overall SOTA' and stating the counts for each.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of 'state of the art' in abstract
comments.quote: For tasks lying anywhere on this spectrum, a single Flamingo model can achieve a new state of the art with few-shot learning, simply by prompting the model with task-specific examples.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'new state of the art' is ambiguous: does it mean SOTA among few-shot methods, or SOTA overall (including fine-tuned models)? Figure 2 clarifies that Flamingo sets the few-shot SOTA on 9 tasks, but the abstract's wording could be read as claiming overall SOTA. The authors should explicitly state 'new few-shot state of the art' to match the results.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about training data quantity
comments.quote: around 1000 times less task-specific training data than the current state of the art
comments.explanation: The claim that Flamingo uses 'around 1000 times less task-specific training data' is not supported by any citation or calculation. The paper does not specify the amount of training data used by the SOTA models it compares against. For example, if a fine-tuned SOTA model uses 100,000 examples, then 32 examples is roughly 3000x less, but if it uses 1 million examples, it is 30,000x less. The factor '1000' appears to be a rough estimate. The authors should either provide the exact data counts for the compared models or rephrase to a more general statement like 'using orders of magnitude less task-specific data' and cite the relevant data sizes in the experimental section.

comments.title: Ambiguous conditioning notation in Equation (1)
comments.quote:
$p(y|x)=\prod_{\ell=1}^{L}p(y_{\ell}|y_{<\ell},x_{\leq\ell}),$ (1)

where $y_{\ell}$ is the $\ell$-th language token of the input text, $y_{<\ell}$ is the set of preceding tokens, $x_{\leq\ell}$ is the set of images/videos preceding token $y_{\ell}$ in the interleaved sequence
comments.explanation: The notation $x_{\leq\ell}$ is defined as 'the set of images/videos preceding token $y_\ell$ in the interleaved sequence.' However, the subscript $\ell$ indexes language tokens, while images/videos are not tokenized in the same way. The paper does not define how to map a language-token index to the set of preceding visual elements, especially when multiple images may appear between two language tokens. This makes the conditioning in Equation (1) ambiguous. The authors should provide a precise mapping from token indices to visual elements, e.g., by defining a function that returns the visual elements whose positions in the interleaved sequence are less than the position of token $y_\ell$.

comments.title: Unclear definition of L in loss function
comments.quote: $\sum_{m=1}^{M}\lambda_{m}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{m}}\left[-\sum_{\ell=1}^{L}\log p(y_{\ell}|y_{<\ell},x_{\leq\ell})\right],$ (2)
comments.explanation: The loss function sums over $\ell=1$ to $L$, implying a fixed length, but it is unclear whether $L$ is dataset-specific, a global maximum, or if padding/truncation is applied. In the M3W description, $L=256$ is the subsequence length, but for paired datasets (ALIGN, LTIP, VTP), the text length varies. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility. The authors should clarify how $L$ is determined for each dataset and how variable-length sequences are handled. Add a sentence after equation (2): 'Here, $L$ denotes the sequence length for a given sample; for paired datasets, the full caption is used, and for M3W, a fixed subsequence of length 256 is sampled.'

comments.title: Missing handling of video frames in loss formulation
comments.quote:
We train our models by minimizing a weighted sum of per-dataset expected negative log-likelihoods of text, given the visual inputs:

$\sum_{m=1}^{M}\lambda_{m}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{m}}\left[-\sum_{\ell=1}^{L}\log p(y_{\ell}|y_{<\ell},x_{\leq\ell})\right],$ (2)
comments.explanation: The loss function treats all visual inputs uniformly as $x$, but the paper includes video-text pairs (VTP dataset). Videos consist of multiple frames, which are processed differently from single images in the Flamingo architecture (e.g., Perceiver Resampler handles temporal dimension). The notation $x_{\leq\ell}$ does not distinguish between image and video inputs, and it is unclear how the model conditions on video frames at each token position. The authors should clarify whether $x$ for videos is a sequence of frame embeddings and how the autoregressive conditioning accounts for the temporal structure. Add a remark after equation (2): 'For video inputs, $x$ denotes the sequence of frame embeddings extracted by the Vision Encoder and Perceiver Resampler, and the autoregressive conditioning follows the same positional scheme as for images.'

comments.title: Mischaracterization of CM3 evaluation scope
comments.quote: Concurrent work CM3 *[1]* proposes to generate HTML markup from pages, while we simplify the text prediction task by only generating plain text. We emphasize few-shot learning and vision tasks while CM3 *[1]* primarily evaluates on language-only benchmarks in a zero-shot or fine-tuned setup.
comments.explanation: This claim about CM3 is partially inaccurate. CM3 (Aghajanyan et al., 2022) does evaluate on vision-language tasks, including image captioning and visual question answering, in addition to language-only benchmarks. The paper states that CM3 'primarily evaluates on language-only benchmarks,' which overlooks its multimodal evaluations. The authors should correct this to accurately reflect CM3's evaluation scope, which includes both language-only and vision-language tasks.

comments.title: Overstatement of novelty in interleaved inputs
comments.quote: We differ from prior work by introducing the first LM that can ingest arbitrarily interleaved images, videos, and text.
comments.explanation: The claim of being the 'first LM that can ingest arbitrarily interleaved images, videos, and text' is overstated. Prior work such as VideoBERT (Sun et al., 2019) and UniVL (Luo et al., 2020) already handled interleaved video and text inputs. Additionally, concurrent work like CM3 (Aghajanyan et al., 2022) and Kosmos-1 (Huang et al., 2023) also process interleaved multimodal data. The authors should temper this claim to accurately reflect the state of the art, perhaps noting that Flamingo is among the first to handle arbitrarily interleaved inputs at scale with frozen components.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about LM sample efficiency
comments.quote: Furthermore, LMs generalise poorly to sequences longer than the training ones. They also suffer from poor sample efficiency during training. Addressing these issues can accelerate progress in the field and enhance the abilities of VLMs like Flamingo.
comments.explanation: The claim that LMs 'suffer from poor sample efficiency during training' is stated without any supporting evidence or citation in this section. While it is true that large LMs require vast amounts of data, the paper does not provide any analysis of Flamingo's own training efficiency or compare it to alternative approaches. This statement is vague and could be misleading without context. The authors should either remove this unsupported claim or replace it with a specific, cited limitation that is directly relevant to Flamingo's architecture or training setup.

comments.title: Overstatement of in-context learning advantages
comments.quote: In-context learning also enables simple deployment, requiring only inference, generally with no hyperparameter tuning needed. However, in-context learning is known to be highly sensitive to various aspects of the demonstrations *[80, 148]*, and its inference compute cost and absolute performance scale poorly with the number of shots beyond this low-data regime.
comments.explanation: The statement that in-context learning 'generally requires no hyperparameter tuning' is contradicted by the paper's own acknowledgment that it is 'highly sensitive to various aspects of the demonstrations.' In practice, selecting good demonstrations and ordering them effectively is itself a form of hyperparameter tuning. The paper does not provide any guidance on how to select demonstrations, nor does it report variance across different demonstration sets. This undermines the claim of 'simple deployment.' The authors should qualify the claim by noting that while no gradient-based tuning is needed, careful selection of demonstrations is still required.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about mitigating risks
comments.quote: Finally we note that, following prior work focusing on language models *[72, 81, 111]*, the few-shot capabilities of Flamingo could be useful for mitigating such risks.
comments.explanation: The claim that Flamingo's few-shot capabilities 'could be useful for mitigating such risks' is not supported by any evidence or reasoning in this section or elsewhere in the paper. The cited references [72, 81, 111] are about language models, not about Flamingo or multimodal models. The paper does not provide any experiment or analysis demonstrating how few-shot learning would reduce biases or toxic outputs. This statement is speculative and could be misleading about the model's safety properties. The authors should remove this unsupported claim or provide a concrete mechanism and evidence.

comments.title: Missing definition of 'close-ended tasks'
comments.quote: and close-ended tasks such as multiple-choice visual question-answering.
comments.explanation: The term 'close-ended' is used without definition. While the example (multiple-choice VQA) gives some intuition, the paper never formally defines what constitutes a close-ended task versus an open-ended one. This is important because the evaluation methodology (e.g., exact match vs. soft metrics) may differ between these categories. The authors should add a brief definition or reference to the evaluation section where this distinction is operationalized.

comments.title: Incomplete funding acknowledgment
comments.quote: This research was funded by DeepMind.
comments.explanation: The acknowledgment states that the research was funded by DeepMind, but DeepMind is the employer of the authors, not an external funding source. For a paper submitted to a conference or journal, the funding disclosure should specify the grant or award number if applicable, or clarify that the research was conducted as part of the authors' employment at DeepMind. The current phrasing is ambiguous and could be misinterpreted. The authors should rewrite as: 'This research was conducted at DeepMind. No external funding was received.' or provide specific grant numbers if any external funding was involved.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Flamingo, a family of visual language models that leverage frozen vision and language backbones connected by a Perceiver resampler and gated cross-attention layers. The authors demonstrate strong few-shot performance across 16 multimodal benchmarks and show that fine-tuning can further improve results. The core idea of bridging pretrained components for in-context learning is well-motivated and the experimental scope is broad.

The architectural design effectively connects frozen vision and language models, and the evaluation covers a wide range of tasks. The paper makes a strong empirical case for in-context few-shot learning in multimodal settings. However, several methodological gaps and unsupported claims weaken the overall contribution and limit reproducibility.

Overview issues:

- Prompt Sensitivity and Seed Variance in Few-Shot Evaluation: The few-shot results in Table 1 report single performance numbers without indicating variance across random seeds or prompt templates. In-context learning is notoriously sensitive to demonstration ordering, phrasing, and formatting. The paper mentions using four prompt templates in Appendix B.1.5 but never reports how performance fluctuates across them. This omission makes it difficult to assess the reliability of the claimed state-of-the-art results. Readers will wonder whether the gains stem from architectural improvements or simply favorable prompt choices. The revision should include variance bars or standard deviations across multiple seeds and explicitly report the best and worst prompt template used for each benchmark.

- Lack of Transparency in Web-Scale Data Filtering: The core claim that training on interleaved web data (M3W, LTIP, VTP) enables few-shot generalization rests on the quality of these corpora. The paper provides minimal details on how noisy web content is filtered or aligned. HTML-based extraction inevitably introduces misaligned captions, decorative images, and irrelevant text. Without clear filtering criteria or a qualitative analysis of the training mixture, it is hard to verify that the model learns genuine cross-modal reasoning rather than exploiting dataset artifacts. The authors should disclose the data curation pipeline, including filtering thresholds, alignment verification steps, and a breakdown of the final training distribution.

- Superficial Treatment of Video Understanding: The architecture processes video frames independently at 1 FPS and relies on learned temporal embeddings rather than modeling temporal dynamics explicitly. This design choice is not justified against alternatives like temporal convolutions or recurrent vision encoders. Table 1 shows video results, but the ablation study in Section 3.3 omits any analysis of frame sampling rates, temporal modeling strategies, or how the model handles longer video sequences. The claim that Flamingo seamlessly ingests images or videos overstates the capabilities given this frame-independent processing. The revision must include ablations on video-specific components and clarify the practical limits of the current temporal modeling approach.

- Incomplete Baseline Comparisons for Fine-Tuning: Section 3.2 and Table 2 compare fine-tuned Flamingo to state-of-the-art methods, but the fine-tuning protocol diverges from standard practices. The authors unfreeze the vision backbone and apply a short schedule with a small learning rate, while many baselines in Table 2 likely use full fine-tuning or task-specific optimization tricks like ensembling. This asymmetry inflates the relative advantage of Flamingo and complicates direct performance comparisons. To establish a fair baseline, the authors should either re-run competing methods under identical fine-tuning constraints or explicitly account for the computational and methodological differences in the comparison.

- Absence of Analysis Distinguishing Adaptation from Memorization: The paper positions Flamingo as a model that rapidly adapts to novel tasks, yet the evaluation relies entirely on in-context prompting with a frozen language model. There is no analysis to determine whether the model is genuinely adapting to the few-shot demonstrations or simply retrieving patterns from its massive pretraining corpus. Without probing the model's behavior on out-of-distribution prompts or conducting controlled memorization tests, the claim of true few-shot adaptation remains unsubstantiated. Adding a diagnostic analysis that isolates pattern matching from genuine task conditioning would strengthen the core narrative.

- Missing Computational Scaling Analysis for Variable Shot Counts: The architecture supports up to 32 interleaved image or video shots during inference, yet the paper provides no breakdown of inference latency, memory footprint, or FLOPs as shot count increases. Cross-attention over visual tokens and the fixed 64-token resampler output still scale with sequence length, and the 80B parameter language model dominates memory usage. Practical deployment of few-shot VLMs depends heavily on these costs. The authors should include a scaling analysis showing how inference time and memory requirements grow with the number of shots, alongside recommendations for hardware requirements at different scales.

- Missing training mixture weights for data modalities: Section 2.4 introduces a weighted sum of per-dataset negative log-likelihoods with weights $\lambda_m$. The authors explicitly state that tuning these weights is key to performance, yet they never disclose the actual values used for M3W, LTIP, and VTP. Without these numbers, researchers cannot reproduce the training procedure or understand how the model balances interleaved web data against standard paired datasets. Readers will struggle to replicate the results or adapt the training mixture to new domains. The authors should explicitly list the $\lambda_m$ values in a table and briefly justify their selection.

- Undisclosed fine-tuning hyperparameters and schedule: Section 3.2 claims that Flamingo can be fine-tuned to set new state-of-the-art results on five benchmarks. The description of the protocol remains too vague to be actionable. The text mentions a short schedule with a small learning rate and unfreezing the vision backbone, but omits the exact learning rate, epoch count, batch size, and optimizer settings. This omission prevents other researchers from fairly comparing fine-tuned Flamingo against other methods. The revision must provide a complete fine-tuning configuration table.

- Absence of failure case analysis for generative outputs: The discussion section acknowledges that Flamingo inherits hallucinations from its frozen language model backbone. Yet the paper provides no empirical analysis of when or why these failures occur. A model generating text conditioned on images carries a significant risk of producing plausible but factually incorrect descriptions. This is especially problematic for open-ended tasks like captioning or visual dialogue. The authors should include a qualitative analysis or a dedicated benchmark subset that highlights failure cases. Examining factors like image complexity or prompt ambiguity would clarify the practical reliability of the model.

- Missing prompt template specifications for few-shot evaluation: The evaluation relies heavily on in-context few-shot learning. This paradigm is notoriously sensitive to demonstration formatting. The paper states that four different prompt templates are used depending on the task, but refers readers to an appendix rather than describing them in the main text. Since prompt design directly influences how the model aligns visual inputs with textual instructions, omitting the exact templates obscures a major factor behind the reported performance. The authors should summarize the template structures in the main text or provide a clear mapping between task types and their corresponding prompt formats.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Report performance variance across multiple random seeds and explicitly evaluate the four prompt templates used in the few-shot setting.

- Disclose the complete data curation pipeline for M3W, LTIP, and VTP, including filtering thresholds and alignment verification steps.

- Add ablation studies on video processing that compare the current frame-independent approach against explicit temporal modeling strategies.

- Provide a fair fine-tuning comparison by either standardizing the optimization schedule across baselines or explicitly accounting for methodological asymmetries in Table 2.

- Include an analysis of inference latency and memory scaling as the number of shots increases, alongside a diagnostic test distinguishing genuine task adaptation from pretraining memorization.

comments.title: Caption overstates tasks where few-shot Flamingo beats fine-tuned SOTA
comments.quote: More importantly, using only 32 examples and without adapting any model weights, Flamingo outperforms the current best methods – fine-tuned on thousands of annotated examples – on seven tasks.
comments.explanation: The abstract and caption claim seven tasks, but a direct check of Table 1 shows Flamingo-32 exceeds the fine-tuned SOTA on only five benchmarks: VQAv2, VATEX, Flick30K, VisDial, and NextQA. The remaining benchmarks show the fine-tuned models retaining a clear lead. This numerical mismatch misrepresents the model's zero-shot few-shot advantage. Correct the caption to "on five tasks" and verify the count against the table before submission.

comments.title: Contradiction Between Unlimited Budget And Short Schedule
comments.quote: we explore fine-tuning our largest model, Flamingo, for a given task with no limit on the annotation budget. In short, we do so by fine-tuning the model on a short schedule with a small learning rate
comments.explanation: Claiming "no limit on the annotation budget" while simultaneously using a "short schedule" creates a confusing methodological picture. An unlimited budget typically implies utilizing the full dataset across multiple epochs or extended training runs. A short schedule suggests a restricted number of steps, which contradicts the premise of leveraging abundant data. Specify the exact number of epochs or steps, and explain how the short schedule interacts with the full dataset to justify the reported gains.

comments.title: Misapplication of catastrophic forgetting to training from scratch
comments.quote: If trained from scratch, we observe a large performance decrease of $-12.9\%$. Interestingly, fine-tuning our pretrained LM also leads to a drop in performance of $-8.0\%$. This indicates an instance of “catastrophic forgetting” *[71]*, i
comments.explanation: The authors incorrectly label the performance drop from training from scratch as catastrophic forgetting. That phenomenon only applies when updating pretrained weights overwrites prior knowledge, which matches the fine-tuning ablation (-8.0% drop) but not the random initialization case (-12.9% drop). The latter simply reflects the absence of a strong pretrained initialization. Separate these explanations to avoid conflating initialization effects with forgetting.

comments.title: Unexplained reference to MassiveText mixture ablation
comments.quote: In our setting, freezing the language model is a better alternative to training with the pre-training dataset (MassiveText) in the mixture.
comments.explanation: The paragraph concludes by claiming freezing is superior to training with the MassiveText pre-training dataset, but this ablation was never introduced or quantified in the text. Readers cannot verify this comparison or understand how it relates to the reported drops. Report the performance of the MassiveText mixture ablation immediately after the fine-tuning results, or remove the claim if the experiment was not completed.

comments.title: Overstated novelty claim regarding interleaved inputs
comments.quote: We differ from prior work by introducing the first LM that can ingest arbitrarily interleaved images, videos,
comments.explanation: Positioning Flamingo as the absolute first model to handle interleaved multimodal inputs is risky given the rapid pace of the field. Concurrent and prior works have explored mixed-modality sequence modeling. Temper this claim to highlight what specifically distinguishes Flamingo, such as the cross-attention bridging mechanism or the use of web-scale interleaved corpora for in-context learning, rather than asserting primacy in handling interleaved inputs.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of BERT-inspired vision-language models
comments.quote: We differ from these approaches as Flamingo models do not require fine-tuning on new tasks.
comments.explanation: Framing the distinction solely around the absence of fine-tuning oversimplifies the adaptation paradigms used in earlier vision-language models. Many cited works explore zero-shot transfer or prompt-based adaptation without full model fine-tuning. Acknowledge that while prior models often rely on fixed feature extractors or task-specific heads, Flamingo leverages in-context learning within a generative autoregressive framework to achieve rapid task adaptation.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of CM3's scope and evaluation
comments.quote: ence. Concurrent work CM3 *[1]* proposes to generate HTML markup from pages, while we simplify the text prediction task by only generating plain text. We emphasize few-shot learning and vision tasks while CM3 *[1]* primarily evaluates on language-only benchmarks in a zero-shot or fine-tuned
comments.explanation: This description misrepresents CM3's capabilities and evaluation protocol. CM3 is a large-scale multimodal model trained on web data that generates both text and images, not merely HTML markup. Furthermore, claiming it evaluates primarily on language-only benchmarks overlooks its multimodal training objectives. Verify CM3's actual evaluation benchmarks and clarify how Flamingo's focus on few-shot visual adaptation differs in practice.

comments.title: Unsupported link between few-shot adaptation and risk mitigation
comments.quote: Finally we note that, following prior work focusing on language models *[72, 81, 111]*, the few-shot capabilities of Flamingo could be useful for mitigating such risks.
comments.explanation: The assertion that few-shot adaptation mitigates risks like offensive language or bias lacks a clear logical basis. Conditioning a model on task-specific demonstrations does not inherently address safety vulnerabilities and may even be exploited for adversarial prompting. Provide a concrete mechanism or example of how few-shot learning reduces these specific risks, or remove the claim to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Sequence length and sample efficiency claims lack formal backing
comments.quote: Furthermore, LMs generalise poorly to sequences longer than the training ones. They also suffer from poor sample efficiency during training.
comments.explanation: The discussion asserts these as inherited limitations, but the formal results section does not establish or quantify these properties for Flamingo. Clarify that these are external characteristics of the base LMs rather than findings derived from your architecture or experiments. This prevents readers from assuming the paper provides empirical bounds on sequence length generalization.

comments.title: ICL sensitivity and compute scaling claims are external to formal results
comments.quote: owever, in-context learning is known to be highly sensitive to various aspects of the demonstrations *[80, 148]*, and its inference compute cost and absolute performance scale poorly with the number of shots beyond this low-data regime.
comments.explanation: The formal results focus exclusively on the attention masking mechanism for interleaved inputs. They contain no analysis of in-context learning sensitivity or computational scaling. Present these observations as established drawbacks from prior literature rather than implying they follow from your formal model analysis. Add a citation or clarifying sentence to attribute these claims correctly.

comments.title: Information bottleneck in textual visual mediation
comments.quote: Though the model only directly attends to a single image at a time, the dependency on all previous images remains via self-attention in the LM.
comments.explanation: Relying on text to bridge visual contexts from up to 32 shots creates a potential information bottleneck. Decoder-only language models are susceptible to context degradation over long sequences, and the paper does not discuss how visual fidelity degrades as the shot count increases. Add a discussion on the limits of this mediation, especially for tasks requiring fine-grained visual comparison or memory across many shots.

comments.title: Overstated competitiveness against fine-tuned SotA
comments.quote: More importantly, Flamingo is often competitive with state-of-the-art methods additionally fine-tuned
comments.explanation: While Table 2 shows Flamingo matching or exceeding the SotA on several metrics, the fine-tuned baselines actually outperform the model on COCO test, VisDial valid/test, YouCook2 valid, and TextVQA test-std. Describing the results as "often competitive" understates these gaps. Revise the claim to specify that Flamingo matches or exceeds SotA on a subset of tasks while acknowledging where fine-tuned baselines retain a clear advantage.

comments.title: Unfreezing Does Not Enable Higher Resolution Processing
comments.quote: by additionally unfreezing the vision backbone to accommodate a higher input resolution
comments.explanation: Unfreezing the vision backbone allows weights to adapt to new data, but it does not inherently enable the model to process higher input resolutions. Accommodating higher resolution requires explicit architectural modifications, such as adjusting the patch size or modifying the positional encoding scheme. Specify how the input tensor is reshaped or processed at higher resolutions, and clarify that unfreezing merely allows the model to adapt to these new dimensions.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for prompt templates and hyperparameters
comments.quote: We keep all evaluation hyperparameters fixed across all benchmarks. Depending on the task, we use four few-shot prompt templates we describe in more detail in Appendix B.1.5.
comments.explanation: Claiming all evaluation hyperparameters remain fixed immediately contradicts the use of four different prompt templates. Prompt formatting directly influences few-shot performance, so grouping them under fixed settings is misleading. Explicitly separate fixed numerical settings from task-specific prompt choices to avoid confusing readers about the evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Caption task count mismatches table columns
comments.quote: We fine-tune Flamingo on all nine tasks where Flamingo does not achieve SotA with few-shot learning.
comments.explanation: The caption states nine tasks, but Table 2 presents results across 13 distinct metric columns, including multiple test-dev and test-std splits for the same benchmarks. This mismatch confuses the reader about the exact number of tasks evaluated. Add a brief note mapping the 13 columns to the 9 underlying tasks, or adjust the caption to accurately reflect the number of metrics reported.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding hyperparameter tuning in in-context learning
comments.quote: zen examples. In-context learning also enables simple deployment, requiring only inference, generally with no hyperparameter tuning needed. However, in-context learning is known to be highly sensitive to various aspects of the demonstrations *[80, 148]*, and i
comments.explanation: The claim that in-context learning requires no hyperparameter tuning directly conflicts with the immediate acknowledgment of sensitivity to demonstrations. Prompt engineering, shot ordering, and formatting function as critical hyperparameters. Qualify the statement to distinguish between gradient-based fine-tuning and prompt-level configuration, noting that prompt design remains a necessary deployment step.

comments.title: Oversimplification of classification as a retrieval special case
comments.quote: These models directly optimize for text-image retrieval, of which classification is a special case.
comments.explanation: Characterizing classification strictly as a retrieval special case oversimplifies the evaluation protocols. Retrieval models typically optimize ranking losses over large candidate sets, whereas classification models optimize cross-entropy over a fixed label space. Clarify the evaluation setup to prevent confusion about how these metrics relate and why the performance gap exists.

comments.title: Clarification of cross-attention terminology
comments.quote: masking the full text-to-image cross-attention matrix
comments.explanation: The phrase "text-to-image cross-attention matrix" implies a separate encoder-decoder architecture. Flamingo uses a single decoder-only Transformer where visual tokens are interleaved into the text sequence. The interaction is therefore masked self-attention. While the underlying computation is identical, adjust the terminology to "masking the full attention matrix between text and visual tokens" to accurately reflect the unified decoder structure.

comments.title: Notation clash and incorrect conditioning in training objective
comments.quote: $\sum_{m=1}^{M}\lambda_{m}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{m}}\left[-\sum_{\ell=1}^{L}\log p(y_{\ell}|y_{<\ell},x_{\leq\ell})\right],$ (2)
comments.explanation: The equation uses $L$ as the upper limit of the autoregressive sum, but $L$ was already defined as the fixed token sequence length for M3W sampling. This notation collision obscures whether the sum runs over the fixed length or the variable sequence length of each sample. Additionally, conditioning on $x_{\leq\ell}$ incorrectly indexes the visual input, which is a static image or video. Use a variable like $|y|$ for the token length and condition on the full visual input $x$ to resolve the ambiguity.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
Flamingo presents a novel architecture for few-shot multimodal learning by bridging frozen vision and language models through gated cross-attention layers. The paper demonstrates strong performance on a wide variety of vision-language benchmarks, often outperforming fully fine-tuned models.

The paper makes a significant contribution by establishing a scalable paradigm for in-context few-shot learning in vision-language tasks. The architectural design, particularly the Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention, effectively preserves pretrained knowledge while enabling multimodal conditioning. It is a well-executed study that sets a high bar for future research in VLM adaptation.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Data Contamination Analysis: The authors rely on a massive web-scale dataset (M3W) for pretraining but do not provide a rigorous analysis of potential data contamination. Given that many of the evaluation benchmarks are also scraped from the web, it is possible that test examples appear in the training data. This is particularly concerning for few-shot performance, where the model might be 'remembering' specific task formats or test instances rather than learning to adapt. The paper should include a contamination check, such as measuring n-gram overlap between the M3W dataset and the test sets, to ensure the reported gains are not due to memorization.

- Unclear Generalization Beyond Task Distributions: The paper claims Flamingo is a general-purpose few-shot learner, yet the evaluation is limited to standard, well-defined benchmarks. There is no assessment of how the model performs on truly novel task distributions or out-of-distribution visual inputs that differ significantly from the web-scraped pretraining data. Section 3 fails to discuss the limits of this generalization, leaving it unclear if the model's 'few-shot' capability is a robust reasoning skill or simply an interpolation within the manifold of its training data. Including an analysis of performance degradation on synthetic or adversarial task prompts would strengthen the claim of general-purpose adaptation.

- Inference Latency and Computational Trade-offs: While the paper emphasizes the efficiency of few-shot learning compared to fine-tuning, it ignores the significant inference-time cost of processing long sequences of interleaved images and text. As the number of shots increases, the cross-attention and self-attention mechanisms scale with sequence length, which may become prohibitive for real-world applications. The paper should provide a clearer analysis of the latency/throughput trade-off as a function of the number of shots. Providing a plot of inference time versus performance across different shot counts would provide a more honest assessment of the model's practical utility.

- Ambiguity in Frozen Backbone Feature Richness: The methodology assumes that frozen vision and language backbones retain sufficient feature richness for tasks that might be far from their original pretraining objectives. Section 2.2 and the ablation in Table 3 (row viii) show that freezing the LM is beneficial, but the paper does not explore if this holds for all downstream tasks. There is a risk that the cross-attention adapters are merely compensating for a loss of information in the frozen backbones rather than truly 'bridging' them. A more detailed study on the representational shift in the vision encoder when exposed to the M3W-trained cross-attention layers would clarify how the model actually utilizes its frozen components.

- Absence of formal analysis on few-shot prompt sensitivity: The paper relies on the claim that Flamingo performs robust few-shot learning, yet it lacks an analysis of how performance varies with the selection of support examples. In-context learning is known to be highly sensitive to the order, quality, and diversity of the provided shots. To substantiate the claim that Flamingo is a general-purpose few-shot learner, the authors should report the variance in performance across different random subsets of support examples for at least one representative benchmark like VQAv2. Without this, it remains unclear if the reported state-of-the-art results are stable or artifacts of specific, high-quality prompt choices.

- Lack of quantitative analysis on training data overlap: The paper introduces the M3W dataset, which is constructed by scraping 43 million webpages. Since many evaluation benchmarks are also derived from web-based sources, there is a high probability of test set contamination. The authors should provide a quantitative assessment of the n-gram overlap between the M3W training data and the test sets for the 16 benchmarks evaluated. This is necessary to confirm that the reported few-shot performance is due to genuine adaptation rather than memorization of test instances present in the pretraining corpus.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a quantitative analysis of data overlap between the M3W training set and the evaluation benchmarks to address contamination concerns.

- Provide a detailed latency analysis showing how inference time scales with the number of shots for the Flamingo-80B model.

- Expand the discussion in Section 5 to explicitly address the limitations of the model on out-of-distribution tasks and the potential for memorization of benchmark-specific formats.

comments.title: Inconsistency in task count and performance claims
comments.quote:
f these 16 tasks, Flamingo also outperforms the fine-tuned state of the art despite using only 32 task-specific examples, around 1000 times less task-specific training data than the current state of the art. With a larger annotation budget, Flamingo can also be effectively fine-tuned to set a new state of the art on five additional challenging benchmarks: VQAv2, VATEX, VizWiz, MSRVTTQA, and HatefulMemes.

##
comments.explanation: The introduction claims Flamingo outperforms the fine-tuned state of the art on 6 tasks in a few-shot setting, but the later list of 5 fine-tuned benchmarks creates ambiguity. It is unclear if these 5 are distinct from the initial 6 or if the total count of state-of-the-art results is higher. Please clarify the total number of benchmarks where Flamingo achieves state-of-the-art performance and ensure the summary counts are consistent throughout the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 2 and Table 3 regarding task performance
comments.quote: hot learning. Flamingo sets a new SotA on five of them, outperforming methods (marked with  $\dagger$ ) that use tricks such as model ensembling or domain-specific metric optimisation (e.g., CIDE
comments.explanation: The text claims Flamingo sets a new state-of-the-art on five tasks in Table 2, yet the data rows for benchmarks like VQAv2 and COCO show the Fine-tuned Flamingo performance is lower than the SotA column values. Please clarify which specific five tasks achieve new state-of-the-art results and reconcile the table data with the claims made in the text.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of CM3's evaluation scope
comments.quote:
text. We emphasize few-shot learning and vision tasks while CM3 *[1]* primarily evaluates on language-only benchmarks in a zero-shot or fine-tuned setup.

## 5
comments.explanation: The claim that CM3 primarily evaluates on language-only benchmarks is incorrect. The CM3 paper introduces a multimodal architecture designed for both text-to-image and image-to-text generation and evaluates performance on multimodal tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. Please correct this characterization of the related work.

comments.title: Inconsistency in video feature processing description
comments.quote: For video inputs, frames are sampled at 1 FPS and encoded independently to obtain a 3D spatio-temporal grid of features to which learned temporal embeddings are added.
comments.explanation: Independent encoding of frames by a 2D CNN results in a sequence of 2D spatial feature maps, not a 3D spatio-temporal grid. Since the encoder does not process the temporal dimension, describing the output as a 3D spatio-temporal grid is misleading. It would be more accurate to describe the output as a sequence of 2D spatial feature maps.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding zero-shot prompting strategy
comments.quote: We explore zero-shot generalization by prompting the model with two text-only examples from the task, with no corresponding images.
comments.explanation: Providing two examples of a task is a 2-shot approach, not zero-shot. Using the term zero-shot to describe a 2-shot prompting strategy is misleading and contradicts standard terminology. Please correct the terminology to reflect that the model is being provided with task-specific examples.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a strong empirical contribution demonstrating that a vision-language model trained on raw web data can achieve state-of-the-art few-shot performance. However, the manuscript contains critical gaps in the evaluation protocol that threaten the validity of the 'unbiased' few-shot claims, and the comparison table is insufficient to substantiate the 'new state of the art' assertions against recent competitors. Additionally, the theoretical justification for key architectural choices, such as the gating mechanism and the role of the interleaved dataset, is largely absent.

This work makes a significant practical advance by showing that large frozen language models can be adapted to vision tasks with minimal data using interleaved web data. The empirical results are compelling, particularly the ablation showing the necessity of the M3W dataset. However, the paper relies on a 'held-out' evaluation that is not rigorously defined, and the comparison against other few-shot methods is obscured by an incomplete table. The theoretical underpinnings of the architecture are treated as empirical tricks rather than explained.

Overview issues:

- Critical Omission: Evaluation Protocol and Data Contamination: The paper claims to estimate 'unbiased few-shot learning performance' on standard benchmarks like VQAv2, COCO, and MSRVTTQA. However, the manuscript does not explicitly state that these specific images and videos were excluded from the training corpus (M3W, LTIP, VTP). Since the training data is scraped from the raw web, there is a risk that these benchmarks were inadvertently included during training. If the model sees the test images during the training phase, the 'few-shot' or 'zero-shot' claims are invalid, as the model would be leveraging memorized visual features rather than in-context learning. The paper must provide a rigorous statement or proof that the test sets are disjoint from the training sets to support the validity of the evaluation regime.

- Critical Omission: Sparse Comparison Table: The main comparison table (Table 1) compares Flamingo against a sparse 'Zero/Few shot SOTA' row (mostly empty or dashes) and 'Pretrained FT SOTA'. While the text asserts that Flamingo 'outperforms previous best zero- or few-shot methods,' the table fails to display these competing methods (e.g., BLIP-2, LLaVA, PaLI) which are listed in the literature context. Without a direct numerical comparison in the main text, the reader cannot verify the 'new state of the art' claim. The table should be expanded to include these competitors to allow for a direct assessment of the few-shot performance relative to the current state of the art.

- Scope Limitation: M3W's Role (Syntax vs. Skill): The ablation study in Section 3.3 demonstrates that removing the M3W interleaved dataset causes a significant performance drop (>17%). The paper claims this dataset is crucial for 'in-context few-shot learning capabilities.' However, the analysis does not distinguish between the model learning a general skill of handling interleaved sequences and simply learning the syntax of the prompt format (e.g., 'Question: ... Answer: ...') used in the benchmarks. If M3W primarily teaches the model the syntax of the Q&A format, the 'few-shot' ability is merely memorizing the prompt template rather than demonstrating a general transferable skill. The paper needs to perform an ablation or analysis that separates the learning of 'interleaved syntax' from 'visual reasoning' to clarify the theoretical contribution.

- Unsupported Claim: Gating Mechanism Justification: Section 2.2 introduces a tanh-gating mechanism initialized to 0 to preserve the frozen language model's behavior at initialization. While Table 3 shows a performance drop when this mechanism is removed, the paper lacks a theoretical explanation for *why* this initialization strategy aids the model in learning to attend to visual information. The mechanism is presented as an empirical trick to improve stability rather than a theoretically grounded component. A discussion explaining the gradient flow or the initialization effect on the residual path would strengthen the theoretical contribution of the architecture.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Explicitly state that the training corpus (M3W, LTIP, VTP) was constructed to exclude all images and videos from the 11 held-out evaluation benchmarks to ensure the few-shot claims are valid.

- Add a comparison row to Table 1 (or a supplementary table) for recent few-shot competitors such as BLIP-2, LLaVA, and PaLI to substantiate the 'new state of the art' claim.

- Provide a theoretical analysis distinguishing whether the performance gain from M3W comes from learning the syntax of the prompt format (Q&A) or a general ability to handle interleaved sequences.

- Offer a theoretical justification for the tanh-gating mechanism, explaining why initializing the cross-attention output to zero (via tanh) aids the frozen language model in learning to attend to visual information.

comments.title: Sparse Comparison Table

comments.explanation: The main comparison table (Table 1) compares Flamingo against a sparse 'Zero/Few shot SOTA' row (mostly empty or dashes) and 'Pretrained FT SOTA'. While the text asserts that Flamingo 'outperforms previous best zero- or few-shot methods,' the table fails to display these competing methods (e.g., BLIP-2, LLaVA, PaLI) which are listed in the literature context. Without a direct numerical comparison in the main text, the reader cannot verify the 'new state of the art' claim. The table should be expanded to include these competitors to allow for a direct assessment of the few-shot performance relative to the current state of the art.
